learn.title: Wave Energy Converters Explained
learn.intro: Ocean waves carry immense, predictable energy that can power coastal grids. Wave energy converters capture this motion to generate electricity, reducing fossil reliance. With 80% of people living near coasts, harvesting waves supports resilient, local renewable supply and diversifies our clean energy mix beyond solar and wind.
learn.c1_text: Point absorbers are floating buoys that move with waves, driving generators. Like a fishing bobber bobbing on water, they convert vertical motion to power. A single 1 MW unit can supply ~750 homes annually, showing how small devices scale into coastal clean energy farms.
learn.c2_text: These structures trap air above seawater; waves compress it through a turbine. Like blowing across a bottle to make sound, moving air spins the generator. Unlike buoys, they are fixed to shore, easing maintenance while capturing consistent wave energy for grid use.
learn.c3_text: India's MNRE supports wave trials off Kerala and Gujarat. The 2019 Vizhinjam pilot tested a 10 kW absorber in monsoon swells. Though small, it proved local feasibility; India's 7,500 km coast holds ~40 GW theoretical wave potential for future DISCOM-scale supply.
learn.stat1: Global wave resource estimated at 29,500 TWh/year

learn.title: Pumped Hydro Storage Basics
learn.intro: Pumped hydro is the world's largest battery, storing excess solar and wind as gravity potential. It provides 90% of global storage, balancing grids when the sun sets. As renewables grow, this century-old technology is critical to avoid blackouts and enable deep decarbonization affordably.
learn.c1_text: When cheap solar floods the grid, surplus electricity pumps water uphill to a reservoir. Like charging a phone overnight, energy is saved as height. A 100 MW plant lifting 1 million m³ 100 m stores ~270 MWh, ready for later use.
learn.c2_text: At peak demand, water rushes downhill through turbines, generating power in seconds. Like a controlled mountain stream turning a mill, it reverses the pump. Round-trip efficiency reaches 70–80%, making it the most deployed bulk storage worldwide today.
learn.c3_text: India has ~4.7 GW pumped hydro, with 50+ GW planned by 2030 under MNRE. The 1 GW Tehri stage-2 aids solar integration in Uttarakhand. Policies treat it as renewable storage, helping DISCOMs meet peak gaps without coal.

learn.title: Solar Inverters Compared
learn.intro: Solar panels make DC power; inverters convert it to usable AC. Choosing string, micro, or hybrid types affects system cost, shading loss, and battery readiness. With India at 90 GW solar, understanding inverters helps homeowners and DISCOMs maximize clean energy and grid stability.
learn.c1_text: One inverter serves a 'string' of panels, like a single power strip for many lamps. Cheap and simple, but shade on one panel cuts all. A 5 kW home unit costs ~₹25,000, ideal for unshaded Indian rooftops with low upfront budget.
learn.c2_text: Each panel gets its own small inverter, like individual chargers per phone. Shade on one panel doesn't affect others, boosting yield 5–25%. Cost is higher (~₹8,000/panel), but monitoring per panel aids O&M in dusty Indian conditions.
learn.c3_text: Hybrid inverters manage solar plus battery, like a smart home hub. Under PM Surya Ghar, Indian homes use them for ₹0.5/unit savings with 2 kWh storage. They enable daytime solar shift to evening, cutting DISCOM peak purchase from costly gas.
learn.stat4: PM Surya Ghar 2 kWh typical storage

learn.title: India RPO for DISCOMs
learn.intro: Renewable Purchase Obligation mandates DISCOMs buy a fixed share of green power. It drives India's 500 GW non-fossil target by 2030. Non-compliance risks penalties, yet meeting RPO cuts emissions and stabilizes tariffs amid coal price volatility for utilities.
learn.c1_text: RPO requires utilities to source % of power from renewables, like a fuel mix law. MNRE sets national trajectory; 2024 overall RPO ~29.9%. DISCOMs buy RECs or direct power. Missing target invites ₹0.50/unit penalty, pushing green procurement.
learn.c2_text: DISCOMs forecast demand, sign PPAs, and surrender RECs yearly. Like a quota cafe buying organic beans, they balance cost and compliance. Weak finances delay payments, but RPO enforcement via SERCs improves renewable developer cash flow in India.
learn.c3_text: Karnataka RPO ~23% with solar carve-out; Rajasthan exceeds 40% due to 18 GW capacity. MNRE's 2023 amendment adds green hydrogen RPO from 2025. States trade RECs on IEX, letting deficit DISCOMs like Tamil Nadu meet obligations efficiently.

learn.title: India EV Policy Guide
learn.intro: India's transport is 40% oil imports; EV policy cuts this via PM e-DRIVE and state perks. Faster adoption lowers urban smog and integrates V2G with solar. Understanding schemes helps buyers save and DISCOMs plan smart charging for a cleaner grid.
learn.c1_text: Launched 2024, PM e-DRIVE gives ₹10,000 crore subsidies for e-buses, 2/3-wheelers. Like a festive discount on clean rides, it replaces FAME-II. Targets 3.8 million vehicles; e-buses get ₹1 crore each, easing city pollution and diesel reliance.
learn.c2_text: States add incentives: Delhi waives road tax, Maharashtra gives ₹5,000/kWh. Like local festival bonuses atop national gift, they boost uptake. Kerala's EV policy targets 1 million EVs by 2030, with charging at every 25 km on highways.
learn.c3_text: Under e-AMRIT, India targets 46,000 public chargers by 2030. DISCOMs in Uttar Pradesh offer ₹4.5/unit off-peak EV tariff. With 2.3 million EVs on road (2024), smart charging can absorb midnight solar, stabilizing India's renewable-heavy grid.

learn.title: Second Life EV Batteries
learn.intro: EV batteries retire at 80% capacity but still store grid power cheaply. Reusing them cuts waste and lithium demand. With India's EV boom, second-life packs can back up solar, delaying new cell manufacturing and supporting circular economy goals.
learn.c1_text: A car battery at 80% health can't drive far but stores home energy, like a worn backpack still carrying books. Second-life costs ~$50/kWh vs $150 new. Repurposing 100,000 packs saves ~5 GWh landfill and critical minerals for India.
learn.c2_text: Packs are racked with inverters for peak shaving. Like a community water tank from old barrels, they discharge at 6 pm. IRENA notes 30% lifecycle cost drop versus new storage when paired with solar farms in warm climates.
learn.c3_text: Tata Power reused 1 MWh EV packs at Mumbai charging hub in 2023. NITI Aayog estimates 120 GWh second-life stock by 2030. Maharashtra policy allows refurbished batteries for DISCOM microgrids, aiding rural solar reliability at low cost.
